     The opening line's "everything above" refers to that document's prose half, which is not bundled. -->

# Build specification

_Everything above describes the system in the language of the person using it. This section is the same system stated exactly, for whoever — or whatever — actually constructs it. **Where the two disagree, this section wins**, because it is the one that gets built._

**Why it exists.** The prose above was written to be read. A build needs a different kind of writing: literal formula text, a decision about every ambiguity, and names pinned to the character. The gap between the two is not cosmetic — the section above says "Inbox" while the first real build produced "📥 Inbox", and since finding an existing database *by name* is the only protection against creating a permanent duplicate, that single ambiguity is enough to end up with two Inboxes and no way to delete either.

Anything genuinely unresolved here is marked **[verify]** and must be checked in-product rather than guessed at.

## The target page — pinned

Everything is built as a child of one page, titled **`My System`**. That page is the scoping anchor for every existence check: a build looks for its databases *under this page*, never across the workspace. A client who already has an unrelated database called "Projects" somewhere else is therefore unaffected.

If the page does not exist, the build creates it. If the client wants it called something else, that is fine — but the chosen title must be recorded in `reflow-config` alongside the bindings, because a later run has no other way to find its own work.

## The status block

`My System` carries one **callout block** near the top, which Claude rewrites on every run. It holds the greeting, what was filed, what was left, and — least visibly and most importantly — when the system last ran successfully.

That last part is the one piece of the whole structure that exists to catch the system failing. A client seeing things in her Inbox reads it as "Claude hasn't got to it yet," which is right on a Tuesday and wrong forever after a scheduled run silently stops. Nothing else would tell her, and by design nobody else can see her system to notice on her behalf.

The build creates this block with placeholder text. The daily run rewrites it; it never has to create it.

**Notices need no structure of their own.** When Claude leaves something out of a filed item for confidentiality, the explanation goes in the **page body of the action it concerns** — where it stays as long as the action does, and where the weekly review finds it. The status block carries only a count and a pointer. This deliberately avoids a sixth database: the note belongs with the thing it is about, not in a separate pile.

## Naming — pinned

Database **titles carry no emoji**: `Inbox`, `Next Actions`, `Projects`, `Someday / Maybe`, `Reference`. The emoji shown throughout this document are set as each database's **icon**, which is a separate thing in Notion. Note the spaces around the slash in `Someday / Maybe` — they are part of the name.

Matching an existing database is **exact, case-sensitive, on the plain title**, scoped to children of the target page and never workspace-wide.

**[verify]** The Phase 1a reference build recorded its databases as "📥 Inbox" and so on. Whether that was the title or the icon is unrecorded. Check it during the next sitting — if the emoji is in the title there, either that build or this spec needs correcting, and the two must not be allowed to drift.

## Property types

| Database | Property | Type | Options / detail |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inbox | Name | Title | — |
| Inbox | Captured | Created time | — |
| Inbox | Note | Text | — |
| Next Actions | Name | Title | — |
| Next Actions | Context | Select | `@Calls`, `@Errands`, `@Computer`, `@Home`, `@Anywhere` |
| Next Actions | Status | Select | `Next`, `Waiting`, `Done` |
| Next Actions | Project | Relation → Projects | dual-property; creates `Actions` on Projects |
| Next Actions | Due | Date | — |
| Next Actions | Defer | Date | — |
| Next Actions | Repeat | Text | — |
| Next Actions | Area | Select | `Household`, `Kids & school`, `Practice (admin)`, `Health`, `Personal` |
| Next Actions | Notes | Text | — |
| Next Actions | Completed | Date | written by the daily run, never by Notion |
| Next Actions | Occurrence | Text | stable across a repeating chain |
| Next Actions | Next? | Formula | expression below |
| Projects | Name | Title | — |
| Projects | Status | Select | `Active`, `On hold`, `Done` |
| Projects | Actions | Relation ← Next Actions | auto-created by the dual relation |
| Projects | Open next actions | Rollup | relation `Actions`, property `Next?`, calculation **Sum** |
| Projects | Outcome | Text | — |
| Projects | Due | Date | — |
| Projects | Area | Select | same five as Next Actions |
| Projects | Stalled | Formula | expression below |
| Someday / Maybe | Name | Title | — |
| Someday / Maybe | Category | Select | `Learn`, `Travel`, `Home`, `Read / Watch`, `Buy`, `Ideas` |
| Someday / Maybe | Note | Text | — |
| Someday / Maybe | Added | Created time | — |
| Reference | Name | Title | — |
| Reference | Category | Select | `Household`, `Accounts & logins`, `Health`, `Travel`, `Contacts`, `Other` |
| Reference | Detail | Text | — |
| Reference | Added | Created time | — |

**Status is `Select`, not Notion's dedicated `Status` type.** Notion's own default for a task-shaped database is the `Status` type, so a build that guesses will guess wrong. The "(default)" noted against `Next` above is a convention Claude follows when writing, not a property setting — `Select` has no default-value concept.

**Select option colours are deliberately unspecified.** A colour difference is **not** a repair — a build that "fixes" colours would churn on every run. (The Phase 1a results claimed options were built "and colors as specified"; no colours were ever specified. That claim is wrong and should not be treated as a baseline.)

## Formulas — literal

Notion formula syntax is version-sensitive, so these are the exact strings, not descriptions of them:

```
Next?     prop("Status") == "Next" ? 1 : 0

Stalled   (prop("Status") == "Active" and prop("Open next actions") == 0) ? "STALLED" : ""
```

`Stalled` returns **text**, never a checkbox or boolean. The boolean form was built during Phase 1a verification and degraded into a brittle string comparison. This matters more than it looks: a boolean `Stalled` passes every "does this property exist?" check while the view filtering `Stalled = "STALLED"` matches nothing — leaving the single most important view in the system permanently empty, which is exactly what a healthy system also looks like.

## Views — complete

Eleven views. Ten are specified below; the eleventh is the default table view Notion creates with every database and which cannot be removed through the connector. **The build adopts the default view of the first database as that database's specified view rather than adding an eleventh** — otherwise every run leaves an unnamed stray. **[verify]** confirm during the sitting that adopting-and-renaming a default view is possible; if not, the count is ten specified plus one unremovable default per database.

Hidden in **every** view listed: `Next?`, `Stalled`, `Area`, `Completed`, `Occurrence`. These are plumbing and the client must never see them.

| # | Database | View name | Type | Filter | Group | Sort |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inbox | To process | Table | none | none | `Captured` ascending |
| 2 | Next Actions | Next actions — by context | Board | `Status = Next` AND (`Defer` is empty OR `Defer` ≤ *build date*) | `Context` | `Due` ascending |
| 3 | Next Actions | Waiting for | Table | `Status = Waiting` | none | `Notes` ascending |
| 4 | Next Actions | Due soon | Table | `Due` ≤ *build date + 14 days* AND `Status` ≠ `Done` | none | `Due` ascending |
| 5 | Next Actions | Later (scheduled to return) | Table | `Defer` > *build date* | none | `Defer` ascending |
| 6 | Next Actions | Done (recently) | Table | `Status = Done` AND `Completed` ≥ *build date − 7 days* | none | `Completed` descending |
| 7 | Projects | Active projects | Table | `Status = Active` | none | `Name` ascending |
| 8 | Projects | ⚠ Stalled — needs a next action | Table | `Stalled` = `STALLED` | none | `Name` ascending |
| 9 | Someday / Maybe | Someday list | Table | none | `Category` | `Added` descending |
| 10 | Reference | Reference library | Table | none | `Category` | `Name` ascending |

**Dates in filters are literal, stamped at build time — and there is no relative-date fallback.** Notion has no moving "today" available through the connector. This was probed to the bottom on 2026-07-22: the view filter language has **no** relative-date capability, and the literal word `"today"` is **accepted without error but stored as a dead value that matches zero rows** — a silently-empty view, no warning. So the "build relative filters that never need re-stamping" option is **off the table**; exact dates are the only thing that works. Views 2, 4, 5 and 6 therefore go stale as the calendar advances, and **re-stamping them is the daily run's job — mandatory, not optional, because there is no self-updating alternative.** An item deferred to a date that has since arrived falls out of view 2 *and* view 5 simultaneously and becomes invisible in both. `update_view` *can* re-stamp an exact date cleanly (verified), so the mechanism exists; what makes it load-bearing is that nothing else can. This is also why the daily run's liveness/staleness warning matters: if the run stops, these views silently rot with no relative-filter safety net.

**View 6 filters on `Completed`, not last-edited.** The earlier version sorted by last-edited because no completion date existed. It does now. This also keeps the build's own verification probes out of the weekly review's opening step — they never get a `Completed` value, so they never appear there.

## The Weekly Review page

A page titled `Weekly Review` (emoji as icon), containing **eight linked views** in this order, matching the eight-step walk: Done (recently) · To process · ⚠ Stalled · Active projects · Waiting for · Due soon · Later (scheduled to return) · Someday list. Steps 6 of the walk covers two views (Due soon and Later); step 8 is a question and has no view.

**[verify]** The reference build carried step labels in its linked-view names. Whether that is required, and what the exact labels were, is unrecorded — check during the sitting.

## Build order — a hard chain

Each link depends on the one before it. Out of order, the failures look like connector faults rather than sequencing mistakes:

> the five databases → the dual relation (Next Actions → Projects) → `Next?` → `Open next actions` rollup (which sums `Next?`) → `Stalled` (which reads the rollup) → view 8 (which filters `Stalled`)

Nothing downstream of a missing link can be built. A build interrupted inside this chain must resume from the first missing link rather than starting over.

## Constraints and choices worth knowing

- **Free-tier and connector-safe only.** Everything here is create / read / update on pages and database entries, plus normal views, relations, and rollups — all free-tier Notion features. Nothing depends on Notion AI or any AI-gated query (workspace-wide semantic search, AI database queries). When Claude needs to read a list, it queries that specific database by its own properties (e.g. "rows in the Inbox database"), which is standard connector behavior — never a global search.
- **Where the database IDs live.** The capture skill refers to these lists by name ("the Next Actions list," "the Projects list"). The mapping from those names to the client's actual Notion database IDs is client-owned configuration, bound during connector setup (Phase 0.5 / the two-layer model in Phase 2). The capture skill itself is written to stay tool-agnostic — this document is where that abstraction ends: it *is* the Notion-specific adapter (Select, Relation, Rollup are Notion field types throughout), and only the name-to-ID binding is separately configured.
- **The calendar boundary.** This structure deliberately does not reinvent a calendar. Something you must be at, at a specific date and time (an appointment, a flight), belongs on the client's real calendar — handled by the calendar connector, not by a Notion database. A task that has a *deadline* but flexible timing ("file the form by Friday") is a Next Action or Project with a `Due` date. Claude routes hard date-and-time commitments to the calendar and deadline'd tasks to `Due`.
- **The inbox gets emptied.** By design, a processed inbox item is cleared, not left as a duplicate of the action it became. Two rows for one commitment is a projection that drifts; the home list is the single source of truth, and an empty inbox is the visible proof the system is current. One caveat carried from verification: the connector has no row-level delete or trash operation, so clearing the Inbox has no automatable path today — it belongs to the planned extension MCP (the Notion API supports it; the connector just doesn't expose it).
- **Computed values are invisible to Claude.** Rollups and formulas — `Open next actions`, `Next?`, `Stalled` — render fine for a human reading the page but come back empty when Claude reads them through the connector (finding F-2). Claude can never read a computed count directly; it observes the *effect* (does this project appear in the Stalled view?) or recomputes from the underlying rows. Any future logic that wants a number off a rollup has to work this way instead.
- **Relative dates in view filters go stale — and the "today" literal silently breaks.** A filter written as "Defer is on or before today" is stored with an actual date baked in, not a moving "today," so the by-context view drifts out of date as the calendar advances. Keeping it current is a maintenance job for a scheduled Claude run, not something the view does on its own. **Do not attempt to dodge this by writing the literal `"today"`** — probed 2026-07-22, the connector accepts it but stores a value matching zero rows, so the view goes permanently and silently empty. Exact dates plus the daily-run re-stamp are the only working pattern.
